channel.title: NYT > Science
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Saudis Reach Deal With U.S. That Could Let Them Enrich Nuclear Fuel
item.description: U.S. lawmakers and Israeli officials have expressed opposition to such a plan, to be announced Wednesday, fearing that the kingdom could use a civilian nuclear project to develop nuclear weapons.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger, Edward Wong and William J. Broad
item.category: Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump shaking hands with Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman Al Saud at the White House last November.

item.title: Deep in This Lake, a Contagious Cancer Is Spreading
item.description: Transmissible tumors have been reported in a handful of animal species, but never before among fish. Is it possible in humans?
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer

item.title: Monkeys Understand Shapes in Almost the Same Way as Humans
item.description: Our geometric intuition, long considered something that sets us apart, seems to be shared with other primates.
item.dc:creator: K. R. Callaway
item.category: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

item.title: ‘Too Rare to Care’? A New Center for Rare Diseases Hopes to Change That
item.description: A new nonprofit wants to streamline gene therapy for diseases often avoided by pharmaceutical companies — making treatment more like a routine procedure than a bespoke drug.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata
item.category: Advanced Research Projects Agency for Health
item.media:credit: Moriah Ratner for The New York Times

item.title: The Best Genetic Risk Tools Don’t Work Equally for Everyone
item.description: Genetic prediction models are poised to revolutionize medicine. But because they have been trained on European DNA, they threaten to widen health care disparities.
item.dc:creator: Emily Baumgaertner Nunn

item.title: Trump Attacks National Academies Over Climate Chapter in Judges’ Manual
item.description: The president joined Republican officials who slammed the National Academies of Sciences for a chapter about climate science in an educational guide for judges.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:description: The National Academies of Sciences building in Washington, D.C.

item.title: The Sewer Knows What You Ate
item.description: During the Covid-19 pandemic, wastewater testing became a virus warning system. Now, scientists are using the approach to understand how communities eat.
item.dc:creator: Simar Bajaj
item.category: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

item.title: As Natural Disasters Expand, a Disaster-Tracking App Is Booming
item.description: Known for tracking California wildfires, Watch Duty is moving beyond that mission. Millions of people are turning to it for help.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:description: An emergency vehicle at the flooding Guadalupe River in Comfort, Texas, on Thursday.

item.title: A Mathematical Tribute to the Soccer Ball
item.description: As the World Cup final approaches, take a moment to appreciate the beautiful symmetries of humanity’s favorite truncated icosahedron.
item.dc:creator: Siobhan Roberts

item.title: How to Eat a 2,000-Pound Fish? These Orcas Choose to Pulverize It.
item.description: Marine biologists in Mexico captured video of a strange maneuver that killer whales used to shred their meal.
item.dc:creator: Alexa Robles-Gil

item.title: What Were the Lotus-Eaters Eating in Homer’s ‘Odyssey’?
item.description: In Homer’s epic, Odysseus’s men eat something so powerful that they forget about home. Which plants could have produced such a soporific, narcotic and amnesiac effect?
item.dc:creator: Jack Truesdale
item.media:description: Blue lotus, Nymphaea caerulea, which botanists say is one plausible candidate for the plant that kept Odysseus’s men from wanting to leave the land of the lotus-eaters.

item.title: Dunes Sagebrush Lizard Stripped of Endangered Status by Judge
item.description: The dunes sagebrush lizard, found only in the oil-rich Permian Basin, was classified as endangered two years ago.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn and Maxine Joselow
item.media:description: The dunes sagebrush lizard is found only in areas of the Permian Basin in southeast New Mexico and West Texas.

item.title: He’s the Last Great Land Artist You’ve Never Heard Of
item.description: Charles Ross spent 50 years building “Star Axis,” a naked-eye observatory in New Mexico. Now his masterwork is ready. How to share it with a changed world?
item.dc:creator: Travis Diehl and Brad Trone
item.category: Los Angeles County Museum of Art
item.media:credit: Charles Ross/Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York
item.media:description: The Milky Way ribbons through the sky above “Star Axis.”

item.title: What the Cyclospora Outbreak Reveals
item.description: Warming is expected to fuel the spread of food-borne pathogens. We might not be prepared.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.media:description: A microscopic view of cyclospora parasite eggs, stained reddish pink, in a lab sample.

item.title: Susumu Tonegawa Dies at 86; Nobel Laureate Showed How Antibodies Fight Infection
item.description: The first Japanese scientist to win the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, he went on to discover how memories are stored, advancing understanding of how the brain works.
item.dc:creator: Dylan Loeb McClain
item.media:description: Susumu Tonegawa in 2005. “Few scientists have reshaped our understanding of biology as profoundly,” Myriam Heiman, the director of the Picower Institute for Learning and Memory at M.I.T., said.

item.title: Cameron Hamilton Poised to Lead FEMA Once More
item.description: Cameron Hamilton defended the agency last year when President Trump talked about eliminating it. Emergency managers hope for more stability if he’s confirmed as administrator.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:description: Just over a year after being fired, Cameron Hamilton is on the brink of assuming the helm of FEMA once again.

item.title: 1,700 H.I.V. Treatment Sites Closed After Trump Aid Cuts, a Study Finds
item.description: New research also shows that children and high-risk adult populations have been particularly affected.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: amfAR, The Foundation for AIDS Research
President's Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief
item.media:description: A health clinic in Johannesburg that was closed in 2025, following cuts in U.S. aid.

item.title: The U.S. Is Pulling Back on Energy Efficiency
item.description: The Trump administration is undoing fuel-saving efforts even as war in Iran, data centers and heat waves are pushing up gasoline and electricity costs.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.media:description: A dishwasher at a General Electric plant in Louisville, Ky. The Energy Department has moved to repeal 17 standards for dishwashers, washing machines, kitchen appliances and more.

item.title: Taylor Farms Expands Iceberg Lettuce Recall to 27 States Amid Cyclospora Outbreak
item.description: The affected products were shipped starting June 29 and have expiration dates through Aug. 3.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett and Julie Creswell
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: Taylor Farms — like other food producers — does not disclose which grocery stores or restaurants it distributes leafy greens or other vegetables to.

item.title: The Latest Texas Floods Tested Warning Systems. This Time, They Passed.
item.description: A lack of sirens and flood forecasting systems made the state’s Hill Country vulnerable to deadly floods last summer. That has changed.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance and Edgar Sandoval
item.media:description: Flood debris blocked a road in Kerrville, Texas, on Thursday.

item.title: Rudolph Marcus, Who Illuminated Electrochemical Processes, Dies at 102
item.description: He received a 1992 Nobel Prize for developing a theory that explained fundamental processes behind photosynthesis, respiration, oxidation and even how fireflies produce light.
item.dc:creator: Dylan Loeb McClain

item.title: E.U. Proposes Changes to Emissions Trading System
item.description: The European Union is facing a green backlash as emissions cuts become harder and require bigger overhauls to industry and livelihoods
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.category: Carbon Caps and Emissions Trading Programs
item.media:description: Europe, which often holds itself up as a climate leader, has been facing political infighting over some of its policies.

item.title: Rare Pair of Improbably Light ‘Super-Puff’ Planets Is Discovered
item.description: They’re less dense than cotton candy, and they will help astrophysicists better understand the most unusual ways giant planets can form.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Dzombak
item.media:description: An artist’s concept of the sunlike star TOI-791 and two giant planets, TOI-791b and TOI-791c, that are roughly the size of Jupiter but much less dense.

item.title: Burning Houses to Save Houses
item.description: We visited researchers seeking ways to make homes safer. They set a lot of fires.
item.dc:creator: Mira Rojanasakul
item.media:credit: Will Crooks for The New York Times
item.media:description: A wildfire test at the Insurance Institute for Business & Home Safety lab in Richburg, S.C.

item.title: Astronomers Find an Atmosphere on a Nearby Earthlike Planet
item.description: It’s the first potentially habitable world known to host an atmosphere, making it a lead contender in the search for life beyond our solar system.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:description: An artist’s concept of the exoplanet LHS 1140b, surrounded by a helium-rich atmosphere. Another rocky planet orbits the same cool red dwarf star in the distance.

item.title: Mary-Dell Chilton Dies at 87; Helped Create First Genetically Modified Plant
item.description: In 1982, she led the research team that figured out how to genetically alter plants, a discovery that would eventually transform global agriculture.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman

item.title: National Academies Report Backs Climate Change Attribution Science
item.description: Attribution science is advancing quickly, researchers said. That could support lawsuits seeking damages for severe events worsened by global warming.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.category: National Academies of Sciences, Engineering and Medicine
item.media:description: A firefighting helicopter at a wildfire burning in the Forest of Fontainebleau near Paris on Monday.

item.title: Will SpaceX’s 13th Test Flight of Starship Be Lucky?
item.description: The 33 engines of the rocket’s booster stage had begun to ignite during Thursday’s launch attempt, but were then shut down. SpaceX will try again on another day.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: SpaceX’s Starship rocket remained on the launchpad after a launch attempt was called off just as the engines started to ignite.

item.title: The F.D.A. Approves a New Pill to Slash Cholesterol Levels
item.description: The new pills can lower cholesterol levels far below what can be achieved with statins, clinical trials have shown.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata
item.media:description: Merck’s cholesterol drug enlicitide will be available as a daily pill prescribed by a primary care physician.
